stem: Two hikers begin walking along a straight trail at the same time. Hiker A starts at mile marker 3 and walks toward higher mile markers at a constant speed of 4 miles per hour. Hiker B starts at mile marker 27 and walks toward lower mile markers at a constant speed of 2 miles per hour. At what mile marker will the two hikers meet?
explanation:
Model each hiker's position as a linear function of time $t$ (in hours).

Hiker A's position: $A(t) = 4t + 3$
Hiker B's position: $B(t) = 27 - 2t$

Step 1: Set the two functions equal and solve for $t$:
$$4t + 3 = 27 - 2t$$
$$6t = 24$$
$$t = 4 \text{ hours}$$

Step 2: Find the mile marker by substituting $t = 4$ into either equation:
$$A(4) = 4(4) + 3 = 16 + 3 = 19$$

Verify with Hiker B: $B(4) = 27 - 2(4) = 27 - 8 = 19$. ✓

The two hikers meet at mile marker 19.

distractorRationale.A: Stops after finding the meeting time: solves $6t = 24$ to get $t = 4$ hours, then reports 4 as the answer instead of substituting back to find the mile marker.
distractorRationale.B: Swaps the two hikers' speeds: sets up $A(t) = 2t + 3$ and $B(t) = 27 - 4t$, solves $2t + 3 = 27 - 4t$ to get $t = 4$, then computes $A(4) = 2(4) + 3 = 15$.
distractorRationale.C: Correct answer: sets $4t + 3 = 27 - 2t$, finds $t = 4$ hours, then evaluates $A(4) = 19$.
distractorRationale.D: Makes an arithmetic error when dividing: solves $6t = 24$ but computes $24 \div 6 = 5$ instead of 4, then evaluates $A(5) = 4(5) + 3 = 23$.
